name: Yamata-no-Orochi (Eight-headed Dragon)
shortDescription: Yamata-no-Orochi is a monstrous eight-headed and eight-tailed dragon in Japanese mythology, slain by the god Susanoo.
description: Yamata-no-Orochi is a colossal serpent or dragon featuring prominently in Japanese mythology, particularly in the legends surrounding the storm god Susanoo. Its name translates roughly to 'eight-forked serpent' or 'eight-headed serpent,' reflecting its most striking characteristic: it possesses eight heads and eight tails. The dragon is often depicted as being enormous, capable of spanning eight valleys and eight hills. Its eyes are described as being as red as winter cherries, and its belly is perpetually inflamed and covered in blood. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the oldest chronicles of Japan, Yamata-no-Orochi terrorized the land of Izumo, devouring one daughter each year of a local family, until Susanoo arrived. Susanoo, exiled from the heavens by his sister Amaterasu, encountered an elderly couple, Kunitsukami, who explained that they were about to lose their last daughter, Kushinada-hime. Susanoo devised a plan to slay the dragon. He instructed the couple to prepare eight vats of strong sake, which he placed in front of the eight heads of Yamata-no-Orochi. The dragon, lured by the scent, consumed the sake and became completely intoxicated. While the dragon was incapacitated, Susanoo attacked and slayed it, severing each of its heads and tails. Within the dragon's tail, Susanoo discovered the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi, a legendary sword which he then presented to Amaterasu as a reconciliation gift. The defeat of Yamata-no-Orochi represents the triumph of order over chaos and the establishment of divine authority in the land. The Kusanagi sword is now a part of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, solidifying the dragon's significance within Japanese imperial mythology and culture. The tale is a foundational myth, highlighting themes of heroism, sacrifice, and the importance of appeasing the gods.

sources.title: An Encyclopedia of Shinto
sources.author: Edited by Joseph Cali and John Dougill
